Glossary About
Early Modern

Immanuel Kant

1724 – 1804

The transcendental turn: apperception, the categories, and the self as the I-think of experience.

Kant does not give us a single treatise called “philosophy of mind.” What he gives us is more ambitious and more difficult to reconstruct: a critical account of finite self-conscious cognition, a critique of rational psychology, a pragmatic anthropology of human mentality, a moral psychology of freedom and respect, an aesthetics of feeling and judgment, and a carefully delimited treatment of divine, holy, angelic, and spiritual minds. The result is not a Cartesian psychology of an inner substance, nor a materialist reduction of mind to body. It is a philosophy of finite rational mindedness: what it is for a being like us to receive representations, synthesize them, judge, say “I,” act under laws, feel pleasure and pain, desire, deliberate, and orient itself toward God, immortality, and possible non-human rational beings.

The most compact thesis is this: Kant relocates the “soul” from the status of a theoretically knowable simple substance to the status of a necessary but limited self-relation of finite cognition and agency. The “mind” is better understood as a structured system of faculties than as a thing discovered by introspection. Yet Kant does not thereby trivialize the soul, God, spirits, or angelic beings. He treats them as belonging to different registers: the human soul as the object of inner sense and as a moral-personal subject; God as the idea/postulate of a supreme intelligence and holy will; angels or finite holy beings as possible rational agents not subject to our conflict between reason and inclination; and spirits as possible but not theoretically knowable immaterial intelligences. This makes them central to the architecture of his philosophy of mind, not detachable pious ornaments.

1. The vocabulary: Seele, Gemüt, Geist, Verstand, Vernunft

A careful reconstruction has to begin with Kant’s German vocabulary. English “mind” flattens distinctions that matter.

Gemüt is often the best candidate for “mind” in the broad psychological sense. It is not simply the soul as substance, and not merely the understanding. It names the representational and faculty-complex in which cognition, feeling, and desire are organized. German Kant lexicographical sources describe Gemüt as the faculty or capacity that combines given representations into empirical apprehension, and as the total field of representations together with cognition, feeling of pleasure and displeasure, and desire.

Seele, “soul,” is more dangerous. It is the term of rational psychology: the thinking subject considered as an object of inner sense. Kant does not deny that we can speak of the soul. He denies that speculative reason can know the soul as a simple, immaterial, incorruptible substance. Through inner sense we know ourselves in time and only as appearance; what the soul is in itself remains unknowable. The psychological idea of the soul can have regulative and practical use, but it cannot be converted into constitutive theoretical knowledge.

Geist, “spirit,” has several registers. In aesthetics it can mean the enlivening principle of the mind, especially in genius and artistic animation. In metaphysical and pneumatological contexts it can mean a pure intelligence separated from bodily community. Kant’s position is not crude disbelief. It is rather that whether the human soul is a spirit in this stronger sense is theoretically unknowable, while moral-practical reason may still give the soul a supersensible significance.

This terminological point has also been emphasized in non-Anglophone and translation-oriented reception. A Portuguese/Brazilian discussion of Gemüt, for example, stresses that the term cannot be safely rendered by one English equivalent because it unifies cognitive, affective, and animating dimensions, whereas Seele and Geist carry more specifically metaphysical or spiritual resonances.

So Kant’s philosophy of mind should not begin with the question, “What substance is the mind?” It should begin with the question, “What faculties and acts make experience, self-consciousness, judgment, and agency possible for a finite rational being?”

2. The basic architecture: receptivity, spontaneity, representation

Kant’s human mind is finite. That means it does not create its objects merely by thinking them. It must be affected. In the Critique of Pure Reason, Kant divides cognition into two stems: sensibility and understanding. Sensibility is receptivity, the capacity to receive representations through being affected by objects; understanding is spontaneity, the capacity to think objects by means of concepts. Objects are given through sensibility and thought through understanding.

This is the foundation of Kant’s philosophy of mind. Human mindedness is not passive sensation plus later verbal labeling. Nor is it pure conceptual activity. Cognition requires both intuition and concept. Sensibility without understanding gives no articulated object of knowledge; understanding without sensibility gives no object at all. Kant’s famous formula is that empty thought and blind intuition are both insufficient, but the deeper point is architectural: our mind is a system of receptive and spontaneous powers.

The understanding is discursive. It does not intuit objects immediately. It thinks by concepts, and concepts are functions of unity in judgment. This is why Kant’s logic of judgment becomes central to his theory of cognition: the same understanding that judges also supplies the pure concepts or categories through which objects of possible experience are unified.

A philosophy of mind extracted from Kant therefore begins with representations, but it cannot stop at representations. It must account for the functions by which representations are ordered, synthesized, recognized, and brought under objective rules. The mind is not a container of ideas. It is a law-governed power of synthesis.

3. Synthesis, imagination, and the unity of experience

Kant’s theory of mind is a theory of synthesis. We do not merely receive a manifold of impressions and then inspect it. The manifold of intuition has to be apprehended, reproduced, and recognized as belonging to one possible experience. The imagination, especially in its transcendental role, mediates between sensibility and understanding by making possible the synthesis of the manifold under rules. The Stanford Encyclopedia’s overview of Kant’s mind rightly emphasizes that cognition requires spatial-temporal processing, conceptualization, synthesis, and unity of consciousness, rather than any simple copy-theory of mental representation.

This point matters for the philosophy of mind because it avoids both empiricist atomism and rationalist intellectualism. Kant’s mind is active, but not divine; receptive, but not merely passive; rule-governed, but not reducible to formal logic. Human experience is possible only because sensibility gives a manifold and the understanding, through synthesis, brings that manifold into the form of objectivity.

This also explains why Kant is not offering ordinary introspective psychology in the Critique of Pure Reason. He is asking what must be true of a mind for object-directed experience to be possible at all. His method is transcendental: from the fact that we have experience of objects, he argues to the conditions that make such experience possible.

4. The “I think”: transcendental apperception

The center of Kant’s philosophy of mind is the “I think.” Kant’s claim is not that every mental state is explicitly accompanied by the spoken sentence “I think.” His claim is that any representation that is to count as mine, and any representation that is to enter into cognition, must be capable of being accompanied by the “I think.” This is the principle of transcendental apperception.

This “I” is not discovered as an inner object. It is the formal unity of self-consciousness required for combining representations. Kant calls pure apperception an act of spontaneity. Empirical consciousness may be scattered; representations may come and go. But for them to belong to one experience, they must be combinable in one self-consciousness. Kant even calls the synthetic unity of apperception the highest point to which one must attach all use of the understanding.

The philosophical payoff is enormous. The self is not primarily a perceived item. It is the formal subject of synthesis. I do not first encounter a soul-object and then attach representations to it. Rather, the possibility of objective experience presupposes that representations can be unified as mine. The self is therefore both indispensable and elusive: indispensable as condition, elusive as object.

This is where many misreadings begin. Kant is not saying that the self is unreal. Nor is he saying that the self is known as a metaphysical substance. The transcendental “I” is necessary for experience, but it does not give us theoretical knowledge of what the soul is in itself.

5. Inner sense and the empirical self

Kant distinguishes transcendental apperception from inner sense. Inner sense is the form in which we intuit our own inner states: time. When I attend to my mental life empirically, I do not know myself as a thing in itself. I know myself as I appear to myself in time. Kant states this point sharply: we know ourselves as appearance, not as noumenal reality.

This creates Kant’s characteristic duality of self-relation. As apperceptive subject, I must be able to unify representations. As empirical object of inner sense, I appear to myself as a temporal sequence of states. The same human being is both active in synthesis and receptive to its own inner appearances. Kant sometimes describes this as self-affection: the mind affects inner sense, but what is thereby intuited is still appearance.

The thinking subject considered as object of inner sense is what Kant calls the soul. The body, by contrast, is the object of outer sense. Rational psychology tries to infer from the mere “I think” to claims about the soul’s substance, simplicity, personality, and immortality. Kant’s critical move is to block that inference.

A Kantian philosophy of mind should therefore distinguish three levels: first, the transcendental unity of apperception; second, the empirical inner life given in time; third, the noumenal or supersensible subject, which may be thought but not theoretically known.

6. The Paralogisms: why rational psychology fails

The Paralogisms of Pure Reason are Kant’s great critique of speculative soul-metaphysics. Rational psychology begins with the sentence “I think” and claims to derive from it a doctrine of the soul. Kant reconstructs its ambitions: the soul is treated as substance, as simple, as numerically identical through time, and as standing in relation to possible objects in space. From these, rational psychology hopes to infer immateriality, incorruptibility, personality, spirituality, and immortality.

Kant’s objection is not that these doctrines are emotionally unattractive or theologically naïve. It is that speculative reason has no legitimate path from the logical form of self-consciousness to the ontological constitution of the soul. The “I” of apperception is the subject of thoughts, but that does not entitle us to conclude that it is a simple substance. The unity of the representation “I” is not knowledge of the simplicity of the subject as a thing.

This is one of Kant’s most important contributions to the philosophy of mind. He shows that first-person unity does not by itself settle metaphysics. The fact that all my representations must be unifiable in one self-consciousness does not prove that the bearer of those representations is a simple immaterial substance. Equally, however, Kant does not prove that the soul is material, composite, or perishable. He denies dogmatic knowledge in both directions.

So the Paralogisms are destructive only with respect to rational psychology as speculative science. They are not a dismissal of the soul as morally, practically, or religiously significant. Kant removes the soul from theoretical possession in order to prevent metaphysical illusion and, in his broader critical project, to make room for practical faith.

7. Empirical psychology and pragmatic anthropology

Kant is also skeptical about empirical psychology as a strict natural science. In the Metaphysical Foundations of Natural Science, he says that nature can be divided according to outer sense, the doctrine of body, and inner sense, the doctrine of soul. But he argues that empirical psychology falls short of proper natural science. The reason is not that mental life is unimportant. It is that inner sense supplies only temporal succession, not the sort of mathematically tractable magnitude found in outer intuition; mental states cannot be isolated, recombined, and experimentally manipulated like physical materials; and introspective observation itself changes the state observed.

This is a methodological limitation, not a declaration that psychology is worthless. Kant’s Anthropology from a Pragmatic Point of View is a sustained empirical-pragmatic treatment of human mental life. It examines self-consciousness, imagination, memory, temperament, character, affects, passions, and social-practical self-formation. Kant’s statement that the capacity to have the representation “I” raises the human being above all other terrestrial living beings is one of the clearest anthropological expressions of his view: the human being is a person because of the unity of consciousness through changes.

This gives us a two-tiered Kantian psychology. At the transcendental level, Kant investigates the a priori conditions of possible experience. At the pragmatic-anthropological level, he studies how human beings actually use, misuse, cultivate, and discipline their faculties. A comprehensive Kantian philosophy of mind needs both.

8. Consciousness, unconscious representations, and obscurity

Kant is often read as a philosopher of self-consciousness, but his theory of mind is not limited to fully explicit consciousness. He also recognizes obscure or “dark” representations: representations that affect the mind without being clearly conscious. This theme is especially important in the Anthropology and in Kant’s treatment of animals, instinct, imagination, and sub-personal mental life.

Recent work on Kant and animal minds emphasizes that Kant can attribute representations, desire, reproductive imagination, and instinctive forms of responsiveness to animals while denying them discursive thought, concepts, judgment, and the “I” of apperception. Other scholarship argues that Kant can allow animals to be acquainted with objects in a non-human way even if human sensible representation is tied to understanding in a distinctively discursive structure.

This point matters because Kant’s philosophy of mind has more layers than “conscious rational judgment.” There can be representations without conceptual cognition; there can be affective and instinctive responsiveness without personality; there can be obscure mental contents without full apperceptive ownership. Human mindedness is distinguished by apperceptive unity and rational self-legislation, not by the mere possession of sensory representations.

9. The full faculty system: cognition, feeling, desire

A Kantian philosophy of mind cannot be reduced to epistemology. The Critique of Judgment gives a broader map: all powers of the soul can be grouped under cognition, feeling of pleasure and displeasure, and desire. Understanding legislates for cognition and nature; reason legislates for desire and freedom; judgment mediates between them and is especially tied to feeling.

This triadic structure is crucial. The mind is not merely an information-processing system. It is also affective and conative. It feels pleasure and displeasure; it desires; it judges beauty; it acts under practical laws. In aesthetic judgment, a representation is referred not to the object for cognition, but to the subject and its feeling of life or mental state.

The practical side is equally important. In moral life, reason is not merely theoretical. It determines the will. The moral law produces a distinctive effect in finite sensibly affected beings: respect. Kant’s moral psychology is therefore not an appendage to his epistemology. It is part of his philosophy of mind because it explains how a finite rational being can be moved by a law it gives to itself.

The comprehensive Kantian mind has at least these dimensions: receptive intuition, conceptual judgment, imaginative synthesis, apperceptive unity, feeling, desire, volition, respect, character, and moral self-legislation.

10. Personhood, dignity, and freedom

Kant’s deepest account of the human mind is not merely cognitive but personal. The capacity to say “I” is not just a psychological milestone. It marks the human being as a person: a being whose representations can be unified, whose actions can be imputed, and whose will can be subject to moral law. In the Anthropology, Kant links the “I” to personal rank and dignity, distinguishing the human being from non-rational terrestrial animals.

In practical philosophy, this becomes autonomy. The finite rational mind is not merely a causal node in nature. It can act under the representation of laws. Freedom, for Kant, is not arbitrary choice; it is the capacity of reason to determine the will according to universal law. That is why moral psychology and philosophy of mind converge. A mind that can judge objectively can also ask whether its maxim can be willed as universal law.

This does not mean that human beings are holy. Kant’s human person is internally divided: sensibility supplies inclinations; reason supplies law; the will is finite and can experience moral constraint. The phenomenology of duty is therefore a clue to the structure of human mindedness. We experience the moral law as an “ought” because our will is rational but not automatically in harmony with reason.

11. Mind and body

Kant’s mature position avoids both dogmatic dualism and dogmatic materialism. The body is the object of outer sense; the soul is the object of inner sense. But because both are appearances, speculative metaphysics cannot legitimately infer an ultimate interaction between two substances called body and soul. Rational psychology’s claim to know the soul as immaterial is invalid; materialism’s claim to reduce the soul to matter is also not established by critical philosophy.

Kant’s pre-critical Dreams of a Spirit-Seer contains speculative discussions of the soul, body, and spirits, including reflections on the soul’s relation to the body and the possibility of spiritual communion. But even there Kant is cautious: he compares private spirit-world systems to dreams and insists that claims about spirits lack the public, common world-character of genuine knowledge.

The critical result is that the human mind is embodied for us because our cognition depends on outer and inner sense. But the ultimate metaphysical relation between the thinking subject and matter cannot be theoretically known. Kantian philosophy of mind is therefore embodied without being reductively physicalist, and spiritual without being dogmatically pneumatological.

12. God’s mind: intellectual intuition and holy will

God is not a theological distraction in Kant’s philosophy of mind. The divine understanding is one of Kant’s most important contrast concepts. Human understanding is discursive: it needs intuitions given through sensibility and concepts by which to think them. A divine or intuitive understanding would not be receptive in this way. For such an understanding, objects would be given, or even produced, through the very representation of them. Kant explicitly contrasts this with our understanding, for which categories have significance only in relation to possible sensible experience.

In the Critique of Judgment, Kant names the contrast between our derivative, image-dependent understanding and an archetypal understanding, an intellectus archetypus. This is not empirical theology. It is a boundary concept that helps us understand the finitude of our own cognition. Our mind is not simply “less powerful” than God’s mind. It is structurally different: receptive rather than productive, discursive rather than intuitive, dependent on space and time rather than creator of its objects through understanding.

Practical philosophy adds a second divine contrast: the holy will. In the Groundwork, Kant says that imperatives do not apply to a divine or holy will because such a will is necessarily in accord with the moral law. The “ought” is out of place where there is no possible conflict between inclination and law.

In the Critique of Practical Reason, God appears as the supreme intelligence and will presupposed by the postulate of the highest good: a moral order in which happiness is proportioned to worthiness. This is rational faith, not theoretical cognition. Kant’s philosophy of religion scholarship rightly emphasizes that God and immortality are postulates of practical reason, warranted by moral need rather than speculative proof.

So God’s mind plays at least three philosophical roles. Cognitively, it marks the idea of intellectual intuition. Practically, it marks the idea of a holy will. Religiously, it grounds the moral postulate of a highest good. In all three respects, Kant uses the divine mind to define the limits and dignity of finite human mind.

13. Angels, finite holy beings, and non-human rational minds

Kant does not develop a full angelology, but he repeatedly uses a category that is philosophically close to what traditional theology often means by angels: finite holy rational beings. These would not be God, because they are finite. But they would not be human in our morally conflicted way, because they would not be tempted by inclinations to violate duty.

This is not incidental. Kant’s moral philosophy depends on a taxonomy of rational agents. There are human beings, whose wills are rational but sensibly affected; there is the divine will, holy without limitation; and there may be finite holy beings whose wills conform to law without the human struggle against recalcitrant inclination. In the Metaphysics of Morals, Kant says that for finite holy beings who are not tempted to violate duty there is no doctrine of virtue in the human sense, because virtue is specifically the strength of a human being’s will in mastering contrary inclinations.

This has a direct philosophy-of-mind implication. The feeling of obligation, the experience of moral constraint, and the need for virtue as self-mastery are not features of rational agency as such. They are features of finite rational agency under sensible inclination. A holy finite mind could be rational and moral without experiencing duty as a struggle. Contemporary scholarship on Kant’s holy will has stressed that the holy will is not a decorative reference to “dear angels” but a structural contrast that clarifies human obligation, moral perfection, and the limits of our practical self-understanding.

If we extract a Kantian account of angelic minds, it must be cautious. Kant gives no empirical psychology of angels. He does not license claims about their perception, memory, or social life. But he gives a formal practical category: a finite rational will not subject to the pathological conflict that makes human morality appear as necessitation. Such a being would be under the moral law, but not under imperatives in the human sense.

This is philosophically rich. It means Kant’s philosophy of mind is not anthropocentric in the crude sense. Human mentality is central to critical philosophy because we must begin from our own cognitive conditions, but Kant’s modal space includes animal minds below discursivity, human minds as finite discursive moral agents, finite holy rational minds, and the divine intuitive-holy mind.

14. Spirits and pneumatology

Kant’s treatment of spirits is often misread as simple mockery because of the ironic tone of Dreams of a Spirit-Seer. That is too crude. Kant does criticize Swedenborgian spirit-claims and speculative pneumatology, but his deeper position is methodological. Claims about spirits do not have the status of public theoretical knowledge. They may have weight in hope, moral orientation, or practical faith, but they do not become speculative science.

Spanish-language scholarship on Kant’s pneumatology makes this point especially clearly. Rovira’s study argues that from 1766 onward Kant places pneumatology under a double restriction: divine spirit and non-human created spirits are excluded from theoretical science, yet the possibility of immaterial beings is not refuted. One may admit such beings without having a rational demonstration of them.

This is exactly the balance needed for a respectful treatment of spirits and angels in Kant. Kant does not say, “These are meaningless theological distractions.” He says, in effect: speculative reason cannot know them; empirical claims about them lack the conditions of objective experience; but practical reason may still require orientation toward immortality, God, and a supersensible moral order.

The philosophical lesson is that Kant’s philosophy of mind is stratified by epistemic status. Human inner experience is empirically available but only as appearance. The transcendental subject is necessary but not object-known. The soul as thing in itself is thinkable but not knowable. Spirits and angels are possible ideas but not objects of theoretical cognition. God is not theoretically known, but is practically postulated as supreme intelligence and holy will.

15. Multilingual reception and interpretive accents

The Anglo-American reception often focuses on apperception, conceptualism, self-consciousness, and the relation between Kant and contemporary philosophy of mind. That work is indispensable. The Stanford Encyclopedia, for example, frames Kant’s view around mental functions, synthesis, representation, consciousness, and the relation between transcendental and empirical self-consciousness.

German lexical and primary-text traditions are especially useful for resisting English flattening. They preserve the differences among Gemüt, Seele, Geist, Verstand, and Vernunft. Without those distinctions, one easily mistakes Kant’s critique of rational psychology for a rejection of mind, or his account of apperception for a theory of an introspectively accessible ego-substance.

French reception often brings out the ontological and phenomenological stakes. Michel Henry’s essay on whether the concept of soul has meaning treats Kant’s critique as a radical transformation of the traditional notion of the soul as simple spiritual substance and links the issue to the being of the “I.” A recent French-language discussion of Sellars, Brandom, and McDowell on Kant’s inner sense and apperception also shows how Kant’s account of the self remains alive in contemporary debates over embodied selfhood and normative mindedness.

Spanish and Latin American discussions add valuable emphases. Work on Kant’s mental activity highlights the role of sensibility as the faculty of receiving representations in intuition. Work on formal intuition stresses that Kant’s account is not a simple opposition between raw nonconceptual intuition and imposed conceptual form; formal intuition helps connect apperception, categories, space, and time. Spanish-language work on the third Paralogism and on pneumatology is also important because it asks not only what Kant destroys in rational psychology, but what he still admits about personhood, immortality, spirits, and the practical afterlife of the soul-question.

Taken together, the multilingual reception supports a broader reconstruction: Kant’s philosophy of mind is not merely an early analytic theory of self-consciousness. It is a theory of finite rational life across cognition, affect, agency, personhood, embodiment, animality, holiness, divinity, and the limits of speculative metaphysics.

16. Extracted Kantian philosophy of mind: principal theses

A comprehensive Kantian philosophy of mind can be formulated as the following theses.

First, mind is fundamentally a faculty-system, not first of all a substance. The best broad term is Gemüt: the organized capacity for representation, cognition, feeling, and desire.

Second, all human cognition requires the cooperation of receptivity and spontaneity. Sensibility gives intuitions; understanding thinks by concepts. Neither alone yields knowledge.

Third, human understanding is discursive. It judges by concepts. It does not intuit its objects into being.

Fourth, the imagination is central because mental life requires synthesis. A manifold must be apprehended, ordered, and made available for recognition under rules.

Fifth, the unity of apperception is the highest condition of cognition. The “I think” must be able to accompany representations, not as an empirical observation but as the formal condition of their belonging to one consciousness.

Sixth, self-consciousness is not self-knowledge of a soul-substance. The transcendental “I” is necessary for experience, but it does not reveal the metaphysical nature of the subject.

Seventh, empirical self-knowledge occurs through inner sense and therefore in time. I know myself as I appear to myself, not as I am in myself.

Eighth, rational psychology fails because it mistakes the logical subject of thought for a knowable metaphysical object. The unity of “I” does not prove that the soul is a simple, immaterial substance.

Ninth, Kant’s critique of rational psychology is not materialism. It blocks speculative proofs of immateriality and immortality, but it also blocks dogmatic reduction of the soul to matter.

Tenth, empirical psychology is possible as observation and anthropology, but not as strict mathematical natural science in Kant’s demanding sense. Mental states cannot be treated like externally manipulable physical magnitudes.

Eleventh, mind includes cognition, feeling, and desire. A Kantian philosophy of mind must therefore include aesthetics, affect, pleasure, pain, will, and moral motivation, not only perception and belief.

Twelfth, the human person is a self-conscious and morally imputable being. The capacity for “I” is tied to dignity, responsibility, and personality.

Thirteenth, finite rational agency is marked by conflict. Human beings experience the moral law as duty because inclination can diverge from reason.

Fourteenth, moral feeling, especially respect, is not a mere empirical sentiment. It is the way the moral law affects a finite sensibly conditioned rational mind.

Fifteenth, embodiment is constitutive of human cognition as we know it. We have outer sense for bodies and inner sense for mental states; both are forms of appearance.

Sixteenth, animal minds occupy a lower but real place in the taxonomy. Animals can have representations, desire, imagination, and instinctive responsiveness, but they lack discursive understanding and apperceptive “I” in the full human sense.

Seventeenth, the divine mind is not a magnified human mind. It is the idea of an intuitive, archetypal understanding and a holy will. It is not bound by our receptivity, discursivity, space, time, or categories.

Eighteenth, angelic or finite holy minds are philosophically intelligible as possible rational wills without the human struggle between moral law and inclination. Kant does not give an empirical angelology, but he gives a formal moral category that can respectfully house such beings.

Nineteenth, spirits and pneumatology remain possible but not theoretically knowable. Kant assigns them to the domain of thought, hope, and practical orientation, not to speculative cognition.

Twentieth, Kant’s philosophy of mind is therefore a philosophy of possible minds under critical limits: animal, human, holy finite, divine, and merely problematic spiritual minds are distinguished by their relation to intuition, apperception, discursivity, embodiment, morality, and freedom.

17. Central tensions for further development

Several tensions remain productive rather than fatal.

The first is self-affection. How can the mind be active in determining inner sense while also receiving itself passively in time? This is one of Kant’s deepest problems because it expresses the finitude of self-knowledge.

The second is the status of nonconceptual representation. Kant seems to require concepts for cognition, but he also allows sensible and obscure representations, including in animals. This has generated modern debates over conceptualist and nonconceptualist readings of Kant.

The third is the relation between transcendental and empirical psychology. Kant denies that empirical psychology is strict natural science in his sense, but modern cognitive science has mathematical and experimental tools Kant did not anticipate. Still, his deeper point remains: no empirical psychology can replace the transcendental question of what makes object-directed experience possible.

The fourth is the philosophy of spiritual minds. Kant denies theoretical cognition of spirits, angels, and God, yet he uses divine and holy minds as indispensable contrast concepts. A Kantian philosophy of mind must therefore develop a disciplined modal taxonomy without pretending to possess speculative knowledge.

The fifth is immortality. Kant rejects rational-psychological proof of the immortal soul, but practical reason postulates immortality in relation to moral vocation and the highest good. That makes immortality a practical-metaphysical issue rather than a theoretical-psychological discovery.

Conclusion

Kant’s philosophy of mind is best understood as a critical theory of finite rational mindedness. Its central subject is the human being as receptive, spontaneous, self-conscious, embodied, affective, desiring, and morally free. But the theory is broader than human psychology. Animals show the possibility of representation without full apperceptive discursivity. God shows the idea of non-receptive intellectual intuition and holy willing. Angels or finite holy beings show the possibility of rational moral agency without human moral struggle. Spirits and the immortal soul mark the boundary between thinkability, hope, practical faith, and theoretical ignorance.

The result is a demanding but powerful philosophy of mind: anti-dogmatic, non-reductionist, formally rigorous, morally serious, and open to a hierarchy of possible minds without confusing that hierarchy with speculative knowledge. Kant does not abolish the soul. He disciplines our claims about it, relocates it within the conditions of self-conscious agency, and preserves its deepest significance in the practical life of reason.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
